perturbation.original_text: Without limiting or qualifying the Sponsor's liabilities, obligations, or indemnities, before the Event, the Sponsor will obtain, at its sole cost and expense, a comprehensive general liability insurance policy from a company acceptable to ANTHEMIC and authorized to do business in the state of Illinois with limits of no less than $1,000,000.00 per occurrence and $2,000,000.00 as an annual aggregate.
perturbation.changed_text: Without limiting or qualifying the Sponsor's liabilities, obligations, or indemnities, before the Event, the Sponsor will attempt to obtain, at its sole cost and expense, a general liability insurance policy from a company acceptable to ANTHEMIC and authorized to do business in the state of Illinois with what it considers reasonable limits.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will obtain' to 'will attempt to obtain' weakens the obligation to procure insurance. Replacing specific limits with 'what it considers reasonable limits' introduces significant ambiguity, potentially leading to inadequate coverage. This contradicts the intent of ensuring financial protection for ANTHEMIC.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Illinois Insurance Code aims to ensure adequate financial responsibility and protection. By allowing the sponsor to determine 'reasonable limits' for insurance coverage, the modified text contradicts the intention of the code to ensure sufficient coverage. The law outlines specific requirements to protect the indemnitee which are undermined by the modification, as the 'reasonable limits' may not be adequate to cover potential liabilities, which would constitute bad faith.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'will obtain' to 'will attempt to obtain' weakens the obligation to procure insurance, and replacing specific limits with 'what it considers reasonable limits' introduces ambiguity, potentially leading to inadequate coverage, undermining the intent of ensuring financial protection. The Illinois Insurance Code (as indicated by the provided list of articles) covers various aspects of insurance, indicating the importance of ensuring adequate coverage and financial responsibility. While specific policy limit requirements are not detailed in the snippet, the ambiguity introduced by the changed text contradicts the overall intention of the code to ensure adequate protection and financial responsibility by setting concrete standards and protecting the indemnitee, and thus a legal contradiction is created.

perturbation.original_text: The Sponsor will also maintain any statutorily required workers compensation insurance.
perturbation.changed_text: The Sponsor will endeavor to maintain workers compensation insurance as it deems necessary.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'will maintain any statutorily required' with 'will endeavor to maintain workers compensation insurance as it deems necessary' makes the obligation to maintain legally required worker's compensation insurance discretionary. This is ambiguous because it's up to the Sponsor to decide what is 'necessary'.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Illinois Workers' Compensation Act mandates that employers must provide workers' compensation insurance for their employees. The modified text makes this obligation discretionary, which contradicts the mandatory requirement of the Act. The 'as it deems necessary' part makes the sponsor able to not comply with the legally required workers compensation insurance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 820 ILCS 305/ Workers' Compensation Act. Home Legislation & Laws Senate House My Legislation Site Map Bills & Resolutions Compiled Statutes Public Acts Legislative Reports IL Constitution Legislative Guide Legislative Glossary Search By Number (example: HB0001) Search Tips Search By Keyword Search Tips Advanced Search Illinois Compiled Statutes Back to Act Listing Public Acts Search Guide Disclaimer Printer-Friendly Version Information maintained by the Legislative Reference Bureau Updating the database of the Illinois Compiled Statutes (ILCS) is an ongoing process. Recent laws may not yet be included in the ILCS database, but they are found on this site as Public Acts soon after they become law. For information concerning the relationship between statutes and Public Acts, refer to the Guide . Because the statute database is maintained primarily for legislative drafting purposes, statutory changes are sometimes included in the statute database before they take effect. If the source note at the end of a Section of the statutes includes a Public Act that has not yet taken effect, the version of the law that is currently in effect may have already been removed from the database and you should refer to that Public Act to see the changes made to the current law. EMPLOYMENT (820 ILCS 305/) Workers' Compensation Act. 820 ILCS 305/1 (820 ILCS 305/1) (from Ch. 48, par. 138.1) Sec. 1. This Act may be cited as the Workers' Compensation Act. (a) The term "employer" as used in this Act means: 1. The State and each county, city, town, township, incorporated village, school district, body politic, or municipal corporation therein. 2. Every person, firm, public or private corporation, including hospitals, public service, eleemosynary, religious or charitable corporations or associations who has any person in service or under any contract for hire, express or implied, oral or written, and who is engaged in any of the enterprises or businesses enumerated in Section 3 of this Act, or who at or pr
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an ambiguity regarding the Sponsor's obligation to maintain worker's compensation insurance. The original text states the Sponsor 'will maintain any statutorily required workers compensation insurance,' creating a clear obligation. The altered text, 'will endeavor to maintain workers compensation insurance as it deems necessary,' makes the maintenance of such insurance discretionary, which contradicts the mandatory nature of the Illinois Workers' Compensation Act, which mandates that employers provide worker's compensation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions the 'Workers' Compensation Act', and although does not go into specifics, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation give appropriate context. The 'as it deems necessary' clause allows the Sponsor to potentially avoid compliance with the Act, creating a direct contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: ANTHEMIC will have the right to participate, at its own cost, in the defense of any such claim through counsel of its own choosing.
perturbation.changed_text: ANTHEMIC may have the option to participate, potentially at its own cost, in aspects of the defense of any such claim, possibly through counsel of its choosing, as deemed practical.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides ANTHEMIC with a clear right to participate in the defense of claims. The modified text introduces several layers of ambiguity. 'May have the option' makes the right discretionary, 'potentially at its own cost' introduces uncertainty about who bears the cost, 'aspects of the defense' limits the scope of participation, 'possibly through counsel of its choosing' questions the right to choose their own counsel, and 'as deemed practical' adds a subjective criterion.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1021.6 addresses situations where a party's actions result in a judgment that benefits others, allowing for attorney fee recovery. The modification introduces ambiguity that could hinder ANTHEMIC's ability to effectively defend itself and potentially recover fees under this statute. The 'as deemed practical' qualification could be interpreted to unreasonably restrict ANTHEMIC's participation, thereby limiting their ability to protect their interests and potentially undermining their ability to benefit from or trigger provisions like CCP § 1021.6 that rely on active and meaningful participation in the defense.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1021.6 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1021.6. Upon motion, a court after reviewing the evidence in the principal case may award attorney’s fees to a person who prevails on a claim for implied indemnity if the court finds (a) that the indemnitee through the tort of the indemnitor has been required to act in the protection of the indemnitee’s interest by bringing an action against or defending an action by a third person and (b) if that indemnitor was properly notified of the demand to bring the action or provide the defense and did not avail itself of the opportunity to do so, and (c) that the trier of fact determined that the indemnitee was without fault in the principal case which is the basis for the action in indemnity or that the indemnitee had a final judgment entered in his or her favor granting a summary judgment, a nonsuit, or a directed verdict. (Amended by Stats. 1982, Ch. 1383, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that weaken ANTHEMIC's right to participate in the defense, which contradicts the principles underlying CCP § 1021.6. While the statute itself doesn't explicitly guarantee participation rights, it contemplates a scenario where an indemnitee actively defends an action. By making ANTHEMIC's participation discretionary ('may have the option'), limiting the scope ('aspects of the defense'), and introducing subjective criteria ('as deemed practical'), the modified text undermines ANTHEMIC's ability to mount an effective defense and potentially benefit from CCP § 1021.6. The original text grants a right to participate, but the changed text introduces uncertainty that limits this ability, impacting the potential recovery of attorney's fees under CCP 1021.6, which requires active defense as stated in the Law Explanation.
